text: Robotic left adrenalectomy for pheochromocytoma. The patient is a young male with clinical radiographic and biochemical signs of a three point five centimeter left adrenal pheochromocytoma. The operation has five main components. Mobilization of the left colon, mobilization of the pancreas and spleen, identification of the renal vein and ligation of the adrenal vein, and finally, dissection of the tissues around the adrenal. This is the only component of the operation which is really non-anatomic.

The surgeon uses the da Vinci XI robotic system to dissect around the adrenal gland, fully mobilizing it. The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position, with the robot docked from the left side. The robotic arms hold two graspers, a camera, and scissors, which are replaced with a vessel sealer. The ports are arranged diagonally from the midline between the umbilicus and xiphoid to the left lower quadrant, with an additional infraumbilical assistance port for suctioning.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection and cautery to mobilize the left colon, identifying a splenial superior to the splenic flexure. The splenial is resected and sent for frozen section confirmation. Dissection continues medially, fully mobilizing the splenic flexure and proximal left colon over Girota's fascia.

The surgeon dissects around the left kidney, identifying the left renal vein. The pancreas is mobilized, and its lateral attachments to the spleen are released, allowing both organs to be reflected medially. The dissection occurs in an avascular plane posterior to the pancreas and spleen, ensuring a bloodless field. As the pancreas is medialized, the adrenal mass becomes visible in the retroperitoneum. Further mobilization of the left colon reveals the inferior mesenteric vein, which is also reflected medially. The left renal vein is clearly identified, and dissection continues along the inferior and medial aspects of the adrenal gland, separating the pancreas from the adrenal to expose the full margins of the enlarged adrenal mass.

The surgeon identifies the splenic vein closely associated with the distal pancreas. The left adrenal vein is visualized, and dissection is performed medially and laterally to safely transect it after placing hemoclips. The origin of the adrenal vein from the renal vein is identified. An accessory adrenal vein from the phrenic vein is also identified and will be ligated later. Locking hemoclips are used to control the left adrenal vein.

The surgeon controls and ligates the accessory adrenal vein before using a vessel sealer to dissect along the left renal vein. Dissection continues around and underneath the adrenal gland, staying outside the adrenal tissues to fully mobilize the gland with the pheochromocytoma. Retroperitoneal attachments are severed with the vessel sealer, exposing the left renal artery above the left renal vein. The adrenal gland and pheochromocytoma are placed in an endocatch bag and extracted through an expanded port.
